key_clauses.501(c)(3) Exemption Confirmation: The IRS confirmed that Wilmette Hockey Association has been exempt from Federal income tax under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code since a ruling issued in April 1975. The organization is classified as a public charity under Section 509(a)(2), not a private foundation.
key_clauses.Donor Deductibility: Contributions made to Wilmette Hockey Association are deductible by donors under Section 170 of the Internal Revenue Code. This confirms the organization's status as a qualifying charitable recipient for tax-deduction purposes.
key_clauses.Form 990 Filing Obligation: The organization is required to file Form 990 annually if gross receipts are normally $25,000 or more, due by the 15th day of the 5th month after the accounting period ends. No Federal income tax return is required unless unrelated business income tax applies under Section 511.
key_clauses.Advisory Nature of Letter: The IRS letter is explicitly characterized as an advisory letter, not a binding ruling. The organization may use this letter as evidence of its tax-exempt status if questions arise regarding its Federal income tax status.

key_clauses.Article 1 - Scope: Design-Builder shall furnish all labor, materials, equipment and services for design and construction of an approximately 47,321 gsf ice rink facility at Real Estate Parcel EC-2C in the Techny Land Development, Northbrook, Illinois. Final drawings and specifications must be approved within ten days of first submittal or either party may terminate.
key_clauses.Article 2 - Time of Completion: Substantial Completion must be achieved by August 1, 2008, based on building permit issuance no later than March 4, 2008. Liquidated damages of $3,700 per day apply after 30 calendar days beyond the Scheduled Substantial Completion Date.
key_clauses.Article 3 - Contract Sum: The fixed-price Contract Sum is $7,745,598.00, subject to additions and deductions as provided in the Contract.
key_clauses.Article 4(a) - Progress Payments: Owner shall pay Design-Builder monthly progress payments within 10 days of receipt of Application for Payment, with 5% retainage withheld, payable by wire transfer.
key_clauses.Article 4(c) - Final Payment: Unpaid balance including all retained sums is due within 10 days after Substantial Completion; Owner may withhold 150% of estimated cost of unfinished work and release monthly as items are completed.
key_clauses.Article 4(e) - Late Payment: Unpaid amounts bear interest at U.S. Bank National Association reference rate plus 3% per annum from due date to date of payment.
key_clauses.Article 5 - Bond: Upon Owner's request before commencement of construction, Design-Builder shall furnish a surety bond for performance and payment obligations; the Contract Sum shall be increased by the full premium if requested.
key_clauses.Article 6 - Changes: All changes must be made via written Change Order signed by both parties; if Owner requests a change proposal and elects not to proceed, Design-Builder is reimbursed for design costs incurred.
key_clauses.Article 7(b) - Warranty: Design-Builder provides a one-year warranty from Substantial Completion for defective workmanship or materials; prior to expiration, both parties shall jointly inspect and Design-Builder shall correct non-conforming work.
key_clauses.Article 8(a) - Liability Insurance: Design-Builder must maintain Workers' Compensation (statutory), Employer's Liability ($1,000,000), Commercial General Liability ($5,000,000 including umbrella), and Automobile Liability ($5,000,000 including umbrella), with Owner, Winnetka Hockey Club, and Wilmette Hockey Association named as additional insureds.
key_clauses.Article 8(b) - Builders Risk: Design-Builder shall maintain all-risk builders risk insurance in an amount not less than $6,894,000 (revised to $5,655,346) with a maximum deductible of $2,500, borne entirely by Owner.
key_clauses.Article 9(a) - Termination by Design-Bui: Design-Builder may terminate if work is stopped 30 days by court order, governmental act, or Owner's wrongful failure to pay after 30 days written notice, and recover all design costs, completed work, overhead, and anticipated profit.
key_clauses.Article 9(b) - Termination by Owner: Owner may terminate for material breach, insolvency, or persistent failure after 10 days written notice; Design-Builder forfeits further payment and must assign all subcontracts to Owner.
key_clauses.Article 9(c) - Termination for Convenien: Owner may terminate at any time for convenience and shall pay Design-Builder all amounts including overhead costs, anticipated profit, and sustained damages.
key_clauses.Article 10(b) - Ownership of Design Docu: Design documents remain the property of Design-Builder or its consultants; Owner receives a perpetual royalty-free license for additions or remodeling, with Owner indemnifying Design-Builder for third-party claims arising from such use.
key_clauses.Article 10(m) - Indemnification: Design-Builder indemnifies Owner from claims arising from performance of the Contract, excluding Owner's gross negligence or willful misconduct; indemnity is capped at the Contract Sum as finally adjusted.
key_clauses.Article 10(q) - Hazardous Substances: Owner warrants no knowledge of hazardous substances on site; if encountered, Owner must remediate within 30 days or Design-Builder may terminate and recover amounts under Article 9(a).
key_clauses.Article 10(r) - Consequential Damages: Mutual waiver of consequential damages including lost income, financing, business reputation, and management productivity; waiver applies to all termination scenarios.
key_clauses.Article 11(a) - Arbitration: All disputes resolved by AAA Construction Industry Arbitration Rules in Chicago, Illinois (revised from Minneapolis); prehearing discovery per Federal Rules of Civil Procedure; award is final and judgment-enforceable.
key_clauses.Article 11(b) - Legal Costs: Losing party pays prevailing party's costs and attorney's fees proportionate to the percentage of the award obtained, up to 100%; fee-shifting voided if prevailing party rejected a higher bona fide settlement offer.

red_flags: Contract date in Article 1 states October 8, 2007 but document header states November 8, 2007 — date inconsistency
Builders risk insurance amount revised downward from $6,894,000 to $5,655,346 — potential underinsurance relative to $7.745M contract sum
Design-Builder retains ownership of all design documents; Owner receives only a license, which could complicate future work with other contractors
Liquidated damages of only $3,700/day may be insufficient to compensate Owner for losses from delayed opening of an ice arena with significant bond obligations
Indemnification cap limited to Contract Sum — may be insufficient for catastrophic bodily injury or property damage claims
Mutual waiver of consequential damages could severely limit Owner's recovery in event of significant delay or defective construction
Termination for Convenience by Owner triggers full anticipated profit payment to Design-Builder, creating financial disincentive for Owner to exercise this right
Owner bears 100% of builders risk deductible ($2,500) despite Design-Builder procuring the policy
Owner required to award separate contracts only to AFL-CIO signatory contractors without Design-Builder's written consent — potential restriction on Owner's contracting flexibility
Arbitration venue originally specified as Minneapolis, Minnesota (Ryan Companies home market) and was revised to Chicago, Illinois — original provision was one-sided
Design-Builder's representative (Erik Magsamen) is named but Owner's representative field is blank — incomplete execution
Final drawings and specifications approval window is only 10 days, after which either party may terminate — very short timeline creating risk of early termination
No mention of Illinois Finance Authority bond requirements, bond trustee consent for construction contract, or lender approval provisions despite project being bond-financed
Document transmitted by fax on January 21, 2008 labeled 'Draft #5' — unclear if this is the executed final version
Allowances total $876,949 within a $7.745M contract — approximately 11.3% of contract sum in allowances creates significant cost uncertainty
Permit cost allowance of $181,680 is based on construction costs — formula basis could result in circular adjustment
No liquidated damages cap specified — Design-Builder theoretically exposed to unlimited per-day damages beyond the LD Date
extraction_notes: Document is a Design-Build Fixed Price Construction Contract — not a bond indenture, ground lease, or operating agreement. Most NSIA governance fields (bond principal, DSCR, trustee, lease expiry, reserve requirements, board vote thresholds) are not applicable to this document type and have been set to null. Contract sum is $7,745,598 for construction of the ~47,321 gsf ice rink facility at Techny Land Development, Northbrook, IL. Document was transmitted by fax on 2008-01-21 and labeled 'Draft #5' — execution status is uncertain. Contract date stated in body is October 8, 2007; document header date is November 8, 2007. Several fields contain OCR artifacts from fax transmission that may have obscured additional terms. No bond financing covenants, lender consent requirements, or trustee provisions appear in this document despite the project being financed with $8.49M Illinois Finance Authority bonds.

event_of_default_triggers: Event of Default by Lessee as referenced in the Lease (full terms in underlying Ground Lease EC-2C, not reproduced in this Short Form)
Failure to surrender Premises in good order, condition and repair upon Expiration Date or Termination Date
Unauthorized assignment or transfer of Lease or leasehold estate prior to completion of Initial Project without Lessor's prior written consent

key_clauses.Section 1 — Leased Premises: Lessor demises and leases to Lessee parcel EC-2C, approximately 5.0+ acres of vacant land on Founders Drive, Northbrook, Illinois (unincorporated Cook County). The full legal description is set forth in Exhibit A and depicted in the ALTA/ACSM survey attached as Exhibit B.
key_clauses.Section 2 — Lease Term: The ground lease term is 60 years, 7 months, and 4 days, commencing January 28, 2008, and expiring August 31, 2068, unless sooner terminated. Early termination may occur on a Termination Date as provided in the underlying Lease.
key_clauses.Section 3(a) — Permitted Use: At all times during the Term, the Premises must be used by Lessee and any other occupant only for the Permitted Use specified in the underlying Ground Lease. No deviation from Permitted Use is addressed in this Short Form.
key_clauses.Section 3(b) — Construction Obligations: Lessee covenants to construct and develop improvements on the Premises consistent with Final Plans and Specifications approved by Lessor, including the Building(s) and all on-site and off-site appurtenant improvements. All construction must conform to approved submittals per the Lease.
key_clauses.Section 3(c) — Surrender of Premises: Upon expiration or termination, Lessee must surrender Premises in good order, free of tenancies, liens, and encumbrances except those permitted under the Lease. Lessee is not obligated to evict tenants holding unexpired Tenant Leases at the time of early termination.
key_clauses.Section 3(d) — Title to Improvements: During the Term, Buildings and Improvements remain property of Lessee or the constructing Tenant under a Tenant Lease. Upon the first to occur of Expiration Date or Termination Date, title to all Buildings and Improvements automatically vests in Lessor without any further act.
key_clauses.Section 3(e) — Assignment Restrictions: Prior to completion of the Initial Project, no assignment or transfer of the Lease or leasehold estate is permitted without Lessor's prior written consent, except to a Permitted Assignee or by leasehold mortgage foreclosure. After Initial Project completion, Lessor consent is not generally required, but original Lessee remains liable unless released per Section 9.1.3.
key_clauses.Section 3(f) — Leasehold Mortgage: Creation of a Leasehold Mortgage does not constitute an assignment requiring Lessor consent. Foreclosure sale or assignment in lieu of foreclosure is also exempt from consent requirements, but the successor assumes all Lessee obligations under the Lease from the date of succession.
key_clauses.Section 5 — No Merger: Fee title held by Lessor and the leasehold estate held by Lessee shall not merge even if acquired by the same person. Merger can only occur upon written instrument executed by Lessor, Lessee, all Leasehold Mortgagees, all Fee Mortgagees, and all other interest holders.
key_clauses.Section 6 — Trustee Exculpation: Chicago Title Land Trust Company acts solely as bare legal trustee holding naked title and has no personal liability under the Lease or Short Form. All parties waive any personal liability against the Trustee and must look solely to the Premises and Project for satisfaction of any claims.

red_flags: Trustee exculpation clause is broad — Chicago Title Land Trust Company holds naked title only; Lessor has no operational responsibility, no management role, and no personal liability, which limits recourse for Lessee in disputes with Lessor.

Automatic title vesting: Upon expiration or termination (including early termination), all Buildings and Improvements — including tenant-constructed improvements — automatically vest in Lessor without any act or instrument, which could represent significant value transfer.

Lessee (North Shore Ice Arena, LLC) is solely owned by Wilmette Hockey Association, Inc., a not-for-profit corporation — concentration of control in a single not-for-profit member with no secondary governance structure visible in this document.
Assignment restriction before Initial Project completion is absolute (requires Lessor consent), creating a potential obstacle to refinancing or restructuring during the construction phase.

Ground lease term of ~60.6 years commencing January 2008 — lease expires August 2068, which extends well beyond the 2008 bond maturity horizon, but the full bond documents are not present in this filing to confirm alignment.
Flood hazard note on survey indicates part of the property is within a Special Flood Hazard Area (Zone AE) per FEMA, which may affect insurance requirements and construction costs.
Exhibit B (plat/survey) is partially illegible due to scan quality — key measurements and title exception notes cannot be fully verified from this document.

The underlying Ground Lease (EC-2C) is not included in this filing — this Short Form is only a memorandum for recording purposes, meaning critical terms (rent, DSCR, reserves, default cures, etc.) are not visible here.

No Society of the Divine Word / Divine Word entity appears as a direct party in this Short Form — the ground lessor is a land trust (Chicago Title), obscuring the beneficial ownership chain that connects to the Techny property.
extraction_notes: This document is a Short Form and Memorandum of Ground Lease only — it is a recording instrument referencing the full Ground Lease EC-2C dated December 1, 2007, which is not included in this filing. As a result, most financial terms (rent, DSCR, reserve requirements, bond details, reporting obligations, board governance thresholds, and full event of default triggers) cannot be extracted. The Society of the Divine Word / Divine Word does not appear as a named party; the beneficial owner of the land trust (Trust No. 1114334) is not disclosed in this document. Survey exhibits (pages 14–43) are largely illegible due to scan quality. Bond-related terms ($8.49M IFA Revenue Bonds) are entirely absent from this document. Confidence rated medium due to core lease structure being clearly readable but substantial reliance required on the unproduced underlying Lease for material terms.

key_clauses.Section 2(a) — Guaranty of Payment and P: Guarantors absolutely and unconditionally guarantee full and prompt performance of all Lessee's Obligations due prior to or concurrently with Substantial Completion, including lien-free completion, payment of all construction costs, and payment of all Rent. The guarantee covers obligations under Article 5 of the Lease regarding construction of the Initial Project at Techny Parcel EC-2C.
key_clauses.Section 2(b) — Cure Obligation / Constru: Upon Lessor notice of Lessee construction default, Guarantors must commence performance within 30 days and achieve Substantial Completion no later than 90 days after the Substantial Completion Date specified in the Lease. Guarantors may use the original contractor or a qualified replacement contractor reasonably acceptable to Lessor.
key_clauses.Section 2(c) — General Default Cure: For any material default by Lessee in Lessee's Obligations, Guarantors must perform within 30 days of Lessor's default notice, using contractors of recognized standing in the Chicago metropolitan area.
key_clauses.Section 4 — Termination: The Guaranty remains in full force until all Lessee's Obligations and Guarantor Obligations are finally and irrevocably paid, performed, or observed. The Guaranty survives any clawback or voidable payment determination, and Lessor must provide written confirmation of termination upon request.
key_clauses.Section 7(e) — Corporate Affiliations /: Wilmette Hockey Association is the sole member of Lessee at execution; Winnetka Hockey Club will receive a 50% membership interest upon IRS determination of Lessee's 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status. Guarantors covenant not to divest ownership interests in Lessee to the extent that doing so would violate the Lease's anti-assignment provisions.
key_clauses.Section 9 — Affirmative Covenants / Fina: Each Guarantor must deliver audited annual financial statements within 180 days of fiscal year-end and other financial information within 30 days of Lessor's reasonable written request, for the entire duration of outstanding Obligations.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(a) — Rights of Lessor: Lessor may unilaterally alter Lessee's Obligations, release Lessee, substitute collateral, add or release guarantors, and assign the Guaranty without notice to or consent of Guarantors. None of these actions diminish Guarantors' liability.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(b) — Continuing Guaranty: The Guaranty is unconditional and is not discharged by any modification to the Lease, release of Lessee, invalidity of the Lease, Lessee's bankruptcy, change of ownership, or any other event. An extremely broad non-discharge provision eliminates virtually all defenses.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(e) — Subrogation Waiver: Guarantors irrevocably waive all rights of subrogation, reimbursement, contribution, and setoff against Lessee and any other person until all Obligations are fully performed. This waiver benefits Lessee and cannot be used to reduce Guarantors' liability.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(g) — Effect of Bankruptcy: The Guaranty survives Lessee's bankruptcy and any disaffirmance of the Lease. Guarantors waive any right to seek a stay or injunction against enforcement of Lessor's rights under the Guaranty in any bankruptcy proceeding.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(m) — Joint and Several Liabili: Guarantors are jointly and severally liable for all Obligations (except representations, warranties, and OFAC compliance, which are several only). Lessor may sue one or both Guarantors, settle with or release one without affecting the other's liability.
key_clauses.Exhibit 1(n) — OFAC / Executive Order Co: Each Guarantor and its beneficial owners, managers, officers, and directors must comply with OFAC Executive Order 13224 and related sanctions laws. A Triggering Event (OFAC listing, money laundering charge, etc.) requires 5-Business-Day notice and 10-Business-Day cure, failing which a default under both this Guaranty and the Lease is triggered.

red_flags: Guaranty is absolute and unconditional with essentially no defenses available to Guarantors — Exhibit 1(b) lists an extraordinarily broad set of non-discharge provisions
Guarantors waive subrogation rights in full (Exhibit 1(e)), meaning they cannot seek reimbursement from Lessee even after performing Lessee's Obligations on Lessee's behalf

extraction_notes: This document is a Chicago Title Insurance Company ALTA Loan Policy (2006) with policy number 1401-008392913-D2, insuring Amalgamated Bank of Chicago as Trustee for a $8,490,000 leasehold mortgage on North Shore Ice Arena. The document contains both the final issued policy (dated January 24, 2008) and the underlying commitment (effective November 19, 2007), along with multiple pro forma versions and endorsements. Key governance terms such as DSCR minimums, reserve requirements, bond maturity, and detailed event of default triggers are not present in a title insurance policy — these would appear in the Bond Indenture, Loan Agreement, or Operating Agreement. The trustee named in this policy is Amalgamated Bank of Chicago, not UMB Bank as referenced in the NSIA context; this discrepancy likely reflects a subsequent trustee substitution and should be verified. Several fields in the policy (mortgage recording date, document number, pro forma zoning endorsement zone designation) are left blank, indicating the document was partially prepared before closing was completed. The ground lease term of January 28, 2008 to August 31, 2068 (approximately 60 years) is clearly established. Flood hazard area exposure and the complex Techny Property Association governance structure are the most significant title-related risk factors identified.

key_clauses.Optional Redemption: Bonds maturing 2028, June 2038, and December 2038 may be optionally redeemed on or after December 1, 2018 at par plus accrued interest from Optional Redemption Fund prepayments. June 2038 bonds have an additional early optional redemption window in 2008–2009.
key_clauses.Extraordinary Redemption (Casualty/Conde: Bonds are redeemable if Net Proceeds from insurance, condemnation, or threatened condemnation sales applied to Loan prepayment exceed $1,000,000. Redemption order is specified by Borrower or, absent direction, in inverse order of maturity.

key_clauses.Section 2 — The Guaranty: Guarantors jointly, severally, absolutely, unconditionally and irrevocably guarantee, as principal obligors (not sureties), full and prompt payment of the Bonds when due and performance of all Borrower Obligations under the Loan Documents. Payment obligation is limited to Guarantors' Funds, expressly excluding Excluded Funds (proceeds from Borrower operations or Project assets).
key_clauses.Section 3 — Loss Indemnification: Guarantors are jointly and severally liable to the Trustee for damages, losses, costs or expenses arising from 13 enumerated acts including fraud, misuse of Loan proceeds, willful misconduct, waste, unauthorized transfers, bankruptcy filings, and actions impairing Trustee's recourse to collateral. This creates broad recourse liability beyond standard payment guaranty.
key_clauses.Section 4 — Continuing Guaranty: The Guaranty is absolute, irrevocable, and continuing; Guarantors waive virtually all surety defenses, including notice of default, marshaling rights, and defenses based on Borrower's incapacity or court-ordered discharge. Guarantors remain liable for any deficiency after foreclosure notwithstanding anti-deficiency statutes.
key_clauses.Section 7 — Subordination and Subrogatio: All indebtedness of Borrower to either Guarantor is fully subordinated to the Obligations; Guarantors cannot make loans to Borrower or accept repayment of Borrower obligations during the Guaranty term. Subrogation rights are waived until all Obligations are indefeasibly paid in full to prevent preference clawback risk in bankruptcy.
key_clauses.Section 17(a) — Revenue Covenant: Guarantors may not demand, receive or accept any transfer of Revenues (including distributions and management fees) from Borrower during a continuing Event of Default or if such transfer would cause an Event of Default; any amounts received must be held in trust and immediately turned over to the Trustee.
key_clauses.Section 23 — Release: The Guaranty is released only upon (a) full payment and discharge of all principal, premium (if any), and interest on the Bonds to the satisfaction of the Trustee, plus all other sums payable under the Loan Agreement and Trust Indenture, and (b) no uncured Event of Default existing under the Loan Agreement. Both conditions must be simultaneously satisfied.

red_flags: Trustee is Amalgamated Bank of Chicago — inconsistent with NSIA context identifying UMB Bank as Trustee; may reflect original 2008 trustee before a subsequent trustee substitution.
Guaranty is 'as principal obligor and not as surety' — Guarantors have virtually no surety defenses and are directly liable as if they were the primary borrower.
Guarantors waive anti-deficiency protections explicitly, remaining liable for any deficiency even where Illinois statute might otherwise extinguish it.
Section 3 creates 13 broad indemnification triggers creating full recourse liability, effectively converting a limited guaranty into a full-recourse instrument in many scenarios.
Guarantors are prohibited from making any loans or advances to Borrower for the entire term of the Guaranty, potentially restricting their ability to support NSIA operations.
Guarantors cannot receive any distributions or management fees from Borrower during any Event of Default period, which could create financial strain on the non-profit hockey clubs.
Trustee has unilateral right to amend, modify, extend or supplement Loan Documents without notice to Guarantors without releasing Guarantor obligations (Section 5) — highly one-sided.
Trustee can release collateral without notice to Guarantors and without releasing Guarantors (Section 5).
Guarantors are required to maintain solvency (own property at fair valuation exceeding debts) for the entire term — ongoing financial covenant with no specified cure period.

Subrogation rights are broadly waived to the extent they could give rise to a Bankruptcy Code preference claim — unusually broad waiver potentially leaving Guarantors with no recourse against Borrower.
Document contains duplicate signature pages (pages 11 and 13) with minor differences in execution marks — potential execution irregularity.
No cap on Guarantors' liability specified; exposure is unlimited up to the full amount of Obligations plus all costs, fees and expenses.

key_clauses.Section 6.2 — Payment Obligations Uncond: The Borrower's obligation to make all loan payments is absolute and unconditional, not subject to diminution by set-off, counterclaim, abatement or otherwise. The Borrower may not suspend payments or terminate the Loan Agreement for any cause, including damage to the Project, changes in law, or Authority default.
key_clauses.Section 8.3 — Debt Service Coverage Rati: Borrower and Guarantors must maintain a Debt Service Coverage Ratio of not less than 1.25:1.00, tested annually on audited financials. Falling below 1.25 requires engagement of an Independent Consultant and implementation of recommendations; falling below 1.00:1.00 constitutes an Event of Default regardless of consultant engagement.
key_clauses.Section 9.7 — Merger, Dissolution and Di: Borrower may not dissolve, merge, or dispose of all or a substantial part of its property without satisfying eight enumerated conditions including surviving entity being a Tax-Exempt Organization, assumption of all obligations, and no existing Event of Default. A 'substantial part' means 10% or more of Total Value in any fiscal year.
key_clauses.Section 9.15 — Indemnification of Author: Borrower provides broad indemnification of Authority, Trustee and related persons against all liabilities arising from use or condition of the Project, violations of agreements or laws, and material misstatements or omissions in offering documents. Indemnification obligations survive repayment of all Bonds.
key_clauses.Section 9.19 — Permitted Additional Inde: Borrower may not incur additional Funded Indebtedness unless an Officer's Certificate confirms the Maximum Annual Debt Service Coverage Ratio was not less than 1.25 for each of the two most recent fiscal years for which audited financials are available. Ordinary course accounts payable and Additional Bonds under the Indenture are also permitted.
key_clauses.Section 9.20 — Sale, Lease, Transfer or: Borrower may not dispose of more than 1% of total property in any consecutive 12-month period without satisfying specified conditions. Separately, disposition of more than 3% in aggregate of Bond Financed Property requires an Officer's Certificate of obsolescence/inadequacy or an Opinion of Bond Counsel confirming no adverse tax impact on Bonds.
key_clauses.Section 9.26 — Lease to Qualified Tenant: Bond Financed Property may only be leased to or used by Qualified Tenants. Use by non-Qualified Tenants is permitted only with an Opinion of Bond Counsel confirming no adverse effect on the tax-exempt status of interest on the Bonds.
key_clauses.Section 10.7 — Authority Limited Liabili: The Authority's liability under the Loan Agreement is strictly limited to its interest in the Loan Agreement, Trust Indenture, and related collateral. No pecuniary liability attaches to the Authority's general credit, and no personal liability attaches to Authority members, officers or employees.
key_clauses.Section 12.1 — Defeasance: Upon full payment or provision for payment of all Bond principal, premium and interest, and all other sums due, all pledged property reverts to the Borrower and the Loan Agreement is discharged, except for certain surviving covenants in Sections 8.1 and 9.19. The Authority must execute release instruments at Borrower's cost upon demand.
key_clauses.Section 2.3(xi) — Guarantor Indemnificat: Each Guarantor independently indemnifies and holds harmless the Authority, Trustee and related persons against all losses arising from the Project's condition or use, material misstatements in offering documents, or material omissions. This indemnification expressly survives repayment of all Bonds and termination of the Loan Agreement.

key_clauses.Section 2.2 — Project Timing: The Company must incur a substantial binding obligation to expend at least 5% of Net Sale Proceeds within six months of Closing. All Sale Proceeds and investment earnings are expected to be fully expended by October 1, 2008.
key_clauses.Section 2.3 — Reimbursement: Sale Proceeds may reimburse prior expenditures by Wilmette Hockey Association ($76,047.25) and Winnetka Hockey Club ($60,436.08) provided official intent was declared within 60 days of expenditure and Closing occurs within 18 months of the later of payment or placed-in-service date. All reimbursed amounts must be Capital Expenditures or Costs of Issuance.
key_clauses.Section 3.1(h) — Debt Service Reserve Fu: At Closing, $633,125 of Sale Proceeds are deposited into the Debt Service Reserve Fund as security for the Bonds. This amount equals the least of 10% of principal, maximum annual P&I, or 125% of average annual P&I.
key_clauses.Section 3.5 — Repair and Replacement Fun: The Company must deposit $5,000 per quarter beginning January 1, 2009 until the fund reaches $100,000; deposits resume if the balance falls below that threshold. Moneys are for capital maintenance of the Project and are not expected to service bond debt.
key_clauses.Section 3.6 — Operation Reserve Fund: Funded from revenue residuals after required bond-related deposits; may be used to cover operating shortfalls or extraordinary repairs. Beginning December 1, 2018, any balance as of that date each year is applied to optional bond redemption.
key_clauses.Section 3.8 — Mixed Use / Private Use Al: The concession area and pro-shop (~$144,690 total cost) are identified as Private Use Portions of the Facility. No Bond proceeds are allocated to these areas to preserve tax-exempt status under IRC Section 141.
key_clauses.Section 4.1 — Arbitrage Rebate Covenant: The Issuer, Company and Guarantors covenant to calculate and pay all arbitrage rebate amounts required under IRC Section 148(f). The Issuer's obligation is limited to amounts available under the Indenture; the Company and Guarantors are backstop obligors.
key_clauses.Section 5.1 — Issue Price: Bonds were sold to Oppenheimer at $8,190,946.67 (par $8,490,000 less OID of $131,891.15 and underwriter's discount of $167,162.18). The composite Bond Yield is not less than 6.3195345% per annum.
key_clauses.Section 5.5 — Issuer Fee: The Illinois Finance Authority receives a one-time fee of $40,470 in connection with issuance; no other consideration (cash or in-kind) may be paid to any person in connection with issuing or carrying the Bonds.
key_clauses.Section 6.1 — 501(c)(3) Status: Both Guarantors (Wilmette Hockey Association and Winnetka Hockey Club) must be and remain 501(c)(3) organizations throughout the bond term. The Company (NSIA LLC) is also represented as a 501(c)(3) organization.
key_clauses.Section 6.5 — Management Contracts: Any management contract with a non-governmental, non-501(c)(3) entity must comply with IRS Revenue Procedure 97-13, limiting term, compensation structure, profit-sharing, and board overlap. Maximum management entity board voting power in Company/Guarantors is 20%.
key_clauses.Section 7.2 — Termination: The Tax Agreement terminates 75 days after Bonds are fully paid and retired or when all Rebate Fund obligations are satisfied, whichever is later. Investment records must be kept for six years post-retirement; GIC records for three years.
key_clauses.Section 7.5 — No Sale of Project: Neither the Project nor any portion may be sold or disposed of prior to the last maturity date of the Bonds. This represents an absolute covenant restricting asset disposition for up to 30+ years.
key_clauses.Section 7.7 — Permitted Changes: Yield restrictions and other covenants may be modified without penalty only upon receipt of a Bond Counsel opinion confirming no loss of tax-exempt status. This is the sole mechanism for covenant modification.

No declaration of official intent to reimburse expenditures was previously made (Exhibit D states this explicitly), yet the document proceeds with reimbursement allocations; this may create a technical compliance risk under Treasury Regulations Section 1.150-2.

Section 7.5 prohibits any sale or disposition of the Project prior to final bond maturity (December 2038), a 30-year lock on asset disposition with no carryout or hardship exception stated in this document.

Operation Reserve Fund excess swept to optional bond redemption beginning December 1, 2018 — this could drain operating liquidity at the expense of operational needs if the fund balance is applied to redemption rather than retained for operations.

The Private Use Portion (concession and pro-shop) costs $144,690 but is explicitly excluded from bond-financed expenditures; if these areas are later re-characterized or expanded, it could jeopardize the tax-exempt status of the entire $8.49M bond issue.

Weighted average bond maturity is 21.566 years against a 120% economic life ceiling of 38.02 years — while within limits, the long bond tail (final maturity 2038) creates extended compliance obligations.

Bond Counsel (Barnes & Thornburg LLP) expressly disclaimed responsibility for advising on collateral tax consequences to the Company and Guarantors (Section 7.1), including alternative depreciation system requirements and change-in-use interest deductibility loss.

No DSCR covenant, financial maintenance covenant, or ongoing financial reporting obligation is present in this document — all financial surveillance would need to be located in the Indenture or Loan Agreement, which are not included here.

Ground lease with Society of the Divine Word (Techny property) is not referenced anywhere in this document; the document references only a leasehold mortgage, leaving ground lease risk unaddressed here.

Mortgagor waives all rights of reinstatement and redemption under Illinois law (Section 27), which is a significant and one-sided creditor-protective provision.

Maximum Obligations cap is stated as $25,000,000 (Section 31(j)) while future advances cap is $10,000,000 (granting clause) and bond principal context is $8.49M — the $25M cap is materially higher than the bond issuance, creating potential exposure if additional obligations are incurred.

Single Asset Entity covenant (Section 15) prohibits Mortgagor from holding any other assets or acquiring interests in other entities until full repayment — extremely restrictive and could limit operational flexibility.
Mortgagor pre-consents to appointment of a receiver upon any Event of Default (Section 22), removing judicial discretion.
Mortgagee has irrevocable attorney-in-fact authority to collect all rents upon Event of Default (Section 18), with no time limit or sunset provision.
Mortgagee may unilaterally file UCC financing statements covering 'all assets' of the Mortgagor without Mortgagor's signature (Section 13(v)), providing extremely broad collateral coverage.

Mortgagor is required to exercise Ground Lease renewal options within 15 days of Mortgagee's written demand, and failure to do so grants Mortgagee the unilateral right to exercise the option — effectively transferring a key leasehold right to the lender.
Mortgagor cannot seek dissolution, winding up, or merger without Mortgagee consent (Section 15(g)), severely limiting governance flexibility.
Document is a leasehold mortgage on a land trust interest, adding a layer of title complexity; the actual beneficial owner (likely Divine Word) acts through Chicago Title Land Trust Company.

The abatement condition (forfeiture upon Default) is one-sided and self-referential — a Default that prevents abatement may itself have been triggered by the cash flow shortfall the abatement was designed to cure.
Year 4 rent ($185,712.12/year) represents a dramatic step-up from Year 3 ($80,000.04/year), more than doubling in one year, which may be difficult for a financially stressed operator to meet.

key_clauses.Section 3 — Interest on Deferred Base Re: Requires Lessee to pay additional rent in the form of interest on the cumulative deferred Base Rent balance starting July 1, 2020, per a fixed schedule through Lease Year 29 (ending 8/31/2037). COVID-19 abatement is capped at $71,500.02 with no extension regardless of how long the pandemic affects operations, creating risk of uncovered losses if disruption continued beyond June 2020.
WHA and WHC guarantors are each liable for only 50% and not jointly and severally — if one guarantor defaults on its share, Lessor cannot pursue the other for the full amount.
Guaranty and additional reporting/contractual obligations expire on the earlier of the Catch-Up Point or September 1, 2038 — if Catch-Up Point is reached early, oversight requirements terminate.

Lease term runs through August 31, 2040 (Lease Year 32) with no explicit renewal option visible in this amendment — long-term operational certainty depends on full lease terms not restated here.

key_clauses.Section 2 / Section 3.1.1 — Restated Bas: The Original Ground Lease Section 3.1.1 is replaced in its entirety with a new 32-year schedule of escalating Base Rent beginning at $16,666.67/month in Lease Year 1, dropping to $2,666.66/month in Lease Year 5 (the relief period), and stepping up through $37,500/month in Lease Years 18-22, with further adjustments in later years. A soil remediation abatement of $100,000.08 total ($1,666.67/month) is available in Years 1-5 provided no Default exists.
key_clauses.Section 3 — Catch-Up Payments: If Cash Available after Debt Service exceeds $50,000 in any fiscal year prior to September 1, 2031, Lessee must pay the excess as a Catch-Up Payment credited as prepayment against deferred Base Rent in reverse order (Lease Years 23 through 19). Lessee may request a waiver to retain funds for major repairs; Lessor's Beneficiary must decide within 30 days, and the decision is final.
key_clauses.Section 4(a) — Deficit Funding by Member: WHA and WHC are each required to contribute 50% of any projected operating Deficit (FY2012-2013 projected deficit of $163,528) as needed to fund operations and satisfy Financing Documents. No member is liable for more than 50%, but failure to fund causing a Financing Documents default constitutes a Ground Lease Default.
key_clauses.Section 4(b) — Annual Financial Reportin: Lessee must deliver nine categories of financial information to Lessor including audited financials, updated cash flow projections, quarterly actual vs. budget statements, tax and bond payment proofs, fund activity statements, and tax-exempt organization filings. This creates ongoing financial transparency obligations running to Lessor (not just to Amalgamated).
key_clauses.Section 4(c) — No Amendment of Financing: Lessee may not amend any Financing Document without Lessor consent; Lessor's consent may not be unreasonably withheld or delayed provided bond payment amounts and interest rates are not increased. This gives Lessor significant indirect control over the bond structure.
key_clauses.Section 4(d) — Signed Ice Contracts: WHA and WHC have contracted for ice time through July 31, 2023 at rates per Exhibit C; individual commitments cannot be reduced without Lessor consent. By May 31, 2023, if Catch-Up Point not reached, new contracts must be in place through at least Lease Year 24 at no less than 2022-2023 rates.
key_clauses.Section 4(e) — Management Company Restri: Lessee cannot terminate Club Sports Consulting Group or any successor without 30 days prior notice and Lessor consent (not to be unreasonably withheld). This restricts Lessee's operational flexibility regarding management.
key_clauses.Section 4(f) — Guaranty by WHA and WHC: WHA and WHC are individually (not jointly and severally) liable for 50% each of Ground Lease obligations under a separate Guaranty of Payment and Performance executed concurrently. Failure to fund leading to an uncured Default enables Lessor to exercise all remedies.
key_clauses.Section 9 — Trustee Exculpation: Chicago Title Land Trust Company acts solely as trustee with no personal liability; all liability is waived by parties claiming rights under the amendment. This is standard land trust exculpation language under Illinois law.
key_clauses.Leasehold Mortgagee Consent: Amalgamated Bank of Chicago as leasehold mortgagee consents to the Second Amendment and subordinates the lien of the Leasehold Mortgage to the terms and provisions of the Second Amendment. This is required to maintain lien priority consistency.

red_flags: Lessee projected negative Cash Available after Debt Service every year from FY2012-2013 through approximately FY2024-2025, indicating structural cash flow deficits requiring ongoing member capital contributions for over a decade

Property taxes in FY2012-2013 budgeted at $327,034 — more than double the $141,000-$166,987 range projected for subsequent years — suggesting a significant prior-year tax catch-up or reassessment driving the need for this amendment

This is the SECOND rent relief amendment (First Amendment February 2010 for construction defects/cost overruns; Second Amendment 2012 for high taxes and operating expenses), indicating persistent financial distress since opening

Lessor's Beneficiary (Divine Word / Society of the Divine Word) has unilateral final decision authority over Catch-Up Payment waiver requests with no appeal mechanism — 'such determination shall be final'

Lessee cannot return any capital contributions to members (WHA or WHC) until all Make-Up Payments are completed and Catch-Up Point is reached, which per projections is not until approximately 2031 — effectively trapping member capital for ~19 years

WHA and WHC ice contracts expire July 31, 2023 — well before the lease term ends in 2040 — creating revenue concentration and renewal risk that is acknowledged by the requirement for new contracts by May 31, 2023
Management company (Club Sports Consulting Group) change restrictions give Lessor effective veto over operational management decisions
Amalgamated Bank of Chicago serves as both bond trustee under the Trust Indenture AND leasehold mortgagee — dual role creates potential conflicts of interest
The DSCR covenant is referenced (Section 8.3 of Loan Agreement) but the minimum ratio is not stated in this document, leaving a key financial covenant threshold undefined here
Trustee (Chicago Title Land Trust Company) has zero personal liability under trustee exculpation — recourse for Lessor is limited to trust assets only

Cash flow projections show the project not generating positive Cash Available after Debt Service until FY2024-2025 (~$9,897), meaning 12+ consecutive years of projected operating deficits funded by member contributions
No mechanism exists for Lessee to terminate the ground lease early without potential forfeiture; combined with member funding obligations, members bear open-ended financial exposure
extraction_notes: Document is a fully executed Second Amendment to Ground Lease dated effective September 1, 2012, with Lessor notarization dated November 6, 2012 and Amalgamated consent dated October 31, 2012. Bond principal of $8.49M per NSIA context — not explicitly stated in this document but referenced indirectly through Financing Documents (Exhibit D). Bond maturity date not stated in this document. DSCR minimum covenant referenced (Section 8.3 of Loan Agreement) but numerical threshold not included here — would require review of the Loan Agreement. Lease term end of August 31, 2040 inferred from Lease Year 32 (9/1/39-8/31/40) being the last year in the Base Rent schedule; Sections 3.1.2-3.1.4 govern Base Rent for the remainder of the Initial Term beyond Year 32 but those sections are not reproduced in this document. The document references 'Lessor's Beneficiary' (Society of the Divine Word / Divine Word per NSIA context) but does not name the beneficiary explicitly in this amendment — identity confirmed only through context. Exhibit B (Cash Flow Projections) is fully included across pages 28-41. Exhibit C (Contracted Ice Hours) is fully included across pages 41-45. Exhibit D (Financing Documents list) is fully included on page 47.

key_clauses.Section 3 / 6.1 — Permitted Use: The Premises must be used solely as an ice skating rink and related facilities consistent with the Techny Overlay District zoning provisions. Beer and wine service is permitted as an ancillary use only, subject to a comprehensive set of conditions including a valid Village liquor license, restricted hours, a dedicated Bar Area with a 25-person maximum capacity, mandatory food service, and per-person drink limits.
key_clauses.Section 6.1(b) — Approvals: Lessee must obtain Lessor's prior written approval before seeking any approvals, modifications, or waivers from the Village or third parties regarding the liquor license or use. Any third-party approvals obtained without Lessor's prior consent are declared null, void, and of no force or effect.
key_clauses.Section 6.1(c) — Lessor's Right to Revok: Lessor retains an unconditional right to revoke the Liquor Sales and Consumption Use privilege if any conditions under Section 6.1 are violated, or if documented complaints arise regarding intoxicated patrons. This revocation right exists notwithstanding any other provision of the Lease.
key_clauses.Section 4 — Reaffirmation: All terms and conditions of the Current Ground Lease as amended by this Third Amendment are reaffirmed and ratified in their entirety. This Third Amendment is incorporated by reference into the Lease.
key_clauses.Section 6 — Conflicts: In the event of any conflict between this Third Amendment and the Current Ground Lease, the terms of this Third Amendment control in all instances and for all purposes.
key_clauses.Section 8 — Entire Agreement: This Third Amendment constitutes the entire agreement between Lessor and Lessee with respect to its subject matter. No prior agreements or understandings pertaining to the subject matter have any validity or force and effect.

Lessor acknowledgment page also appears blank (no name or date filled in), further raising execution completeness concerns

The document references Society of the Divine Word / Techny property in zoning context but the Lessor is Parcel EC-2C LLC — chain of title from Divine Word to current Lessor is not explicitly documented within this amendment
Violations of the liquor use conditions constitute a Default under the entire Lease — a minor operational infraction (e.g., brief food service gap) could trigger full Lease default remedies
Bar Area limited to 25 persons maximum capacity — highly restrictive and could limit revenue potential from ancillary liquor sales
No definition of 'documented, bona fide complaints' provided, leaving Lessor significant discretion to revoke the liquor use privilege

key_clauses.Section 2 — Amended Base Rent Schedule (: Replaces prior base rent schedule in its entirety with a 32-lease-year schedule running from September 1, 2008 through August 31, 2040, with rent starting at $200,000/year and stepping up to a peak of approximately $499,999/year before tapering. Rents in early years reflect prior abatements and deferrals negotiated in earlier amendments.
key_clauses.Section 2 — Soil Remediation Abatement (: Provides a total $100,000.08 abatement of Base Rent over Lease Years 1–5 as accommodation for Lessee's soil remediation costs on Parcel EC-2C. Abatement is forfeited if a Default exists at the time any monthly abatement is due.
key_clauses.Section 2 — COVID-19 Abatement (Section: Grants Lessee a total $71,500.02 abatement of Base Rent for April, May, and June 2020 ($23,833.34/month) due to cancellation or shortening of Spring 2020 youth hockey league caused by COVID-19. Abatement is capped and forfeited if a Default exists; no further relief is available if pandemic extends beyond June 30, 2020.
key_clauses.Section 3 — Interest on Deferred Base Re: Requires Lessee to pay interest as Additional Rent on cumulative deferred Base Rent beginning September 1, 2020, per a fixed schedule running through Lease Year 29 (through August 31, 2037). Interest obligations are relieved prospectively on a lease-year-by-lease-year basis as Catch-Up Payments are made in reverse order from Lease Year 30 back to Lease Year 24.
key_clauses.Section 4 — Catch-Up Payments (Amended S: Requires Lessee to pay to Lessor any Cash Available after Debt Service exceeding $50,000 per fiscal year as a Catch-Up Payment, credited in reverse order against deferred Base Rent for Lease Years 30 through 24 until the total Catch-Up Point of $498,867.02 is reached. If Spring 2020 hockey league revenues are actually received, those revenues must be remitted to Lessor up to the COVID-19 Abatement amount.
key_clauses.Section 4(b) — Catch-Up Payment Relief R: Lessee may request in writing a waiver of a Catch-Up Payment if needed to fund capital repairs not coverable by the Building Repair Reserve Fund; Lessor's Beneficiary must meet with Lessee and decide within 30 days, with the decision being final. If granted, funds must be used strictly for stated purposes and a completion report is required.
key_clauses.Section 5 — Additional Requirements Exte: Extends the expiration of the additional operational and reporting requirements imposed by the Second Amendment from September 1, 2031 to September 1, 2038, and updates the WHA/WHC ice contract renewal evidence deadline to May 31, 2023 for contracts running through Lease Year 32.
key_clauses.Section 6 / Joinder — WHA and WHC Guaran: WHA and WHC reaffirm their Existing Guaranty (from the Second Amendment) as extended to cover obligations under the Fourth Amendment, each severally liable for 50% only (not joint and several). Guaranty obligations continue until the Catch-Up Point is reached or September 1, 2038, whichever is first, provided no Default then exists.
key_clauses.Leasehold Mortgagee Consent — UMB Bank: UMB Bank, N.A. as successor trustee and mortgagee consents to the Fourth Amendment and subordinates the Leasehold Mortgage lien to the terms of the Ground Lease as amended. UMB succeeded Amalgamated Bank of Chicago as mortgagee pursuant to an assignment recorded June 24, 2020.

red_flags: Cumulative deferred Base Rent of $498,867.02 is a significant landlord concession that has accrued over multiple amendments since 2010, suggesting persistent and recurring financial distress at NSIA
COVID-19 abatement is strictly capped at $71,500.02 with no provision for further relief if the pandemic extends or revenues are worse than projected — risk entirely on Lessee beyond June 30, 2020

Updated Cash Flow Projections (Exhibit B) were prepared before COVID-19 impacts and explicitly do not reflect anticipated revenue reductions — projections may be materially unreliable at time of signing

Catch-Up Payment trigger ($50,000 cash available after debt service threshold) is low relative to the scale of deferred rent ($498,867) and bond obligations ($8.49M), suggesting deferred amounts may never be fully recovered

WHA and WHC guaranty is several only (each 50%), not joint and several — Lessor has limited recourse if either guarantor fails to perform, and a partial guaranty failure could trigger an uncured Default

Guaranty and additional requirements expire at Catch-Up Point or September 1, 2038 only if Lessee is not in Default — a Default near the expiry date could reset or extend obligations in an ambiguous manner

Interest rate on deferred Base Rent is not stated explicitly as a percentage in the document; the schedule of dollar amounts implies a very low effective rate, which may undercompensate Lessor for the time value of money
Lessor's 30-day decision on Catch-Up Payment waiver requests is final with no appeal or arbitration mechanism for Lessee if denied
UMB Bank consent was signed/effective April 24, 2020 but not recorded until June 24, 2020 — gap creates a period of potential recording priority ambiguity
No explicit DSCR covenant or bond-level financial covenant is restated or referenced in this amendment, creating a gap between bond indenture obligations and ground lease governance

Footnote 1 notes that interest for three months of COVID-19 deferred rent is paid over only two months in Lease Year 12 — unusual accounting treatment that could cause confusion in payment reconciliation
This is the fourth amendment in 13 years (2007–2020), all providing Lessee relief — pattern suggests structural revenue insufficiency and ongoing dependence on landlord forbearance
extraction_notes: Document is a well-structured ground lease amendment with clear schedules and defined terms. Bond principal of $8.49M and bond maturity date are not stated in this document but are drawn from NSIA context provided. Lease expiry inferred as August 31, 2040 based on Lease Year 32 (9/1/39–8/31/40) being the final year in the Base Rent schedule. DSCR minimum and reserve fund formula are not specified in this amendment. Exhibit B (Updated Cash Flow Projections) pages are blank/not provided in the scanned document. Governing law is Illinois. The document references but does not reproduce the full terms of the Original Ground Lease, Second Amendment Section 4 operational covenants, or the Existing Guaranty — those documents would need to be reviewed for complete covenant extraction.

key_clauses.Section 2 — Escrowed Ground Rent Payment: Lessor will hold Base Rent payments from September 2024 through approximately June 2025 in a segregated Escrow Account up to a total of $300,000, rather than applying them to Lessor's operating account. key_clauses.Section 6 — Other Terms Applicable to Es: Lessee may not request disbursement during any Default; Lessor may apply Escrowed Funds to cure a Default. Lessor's escrow obligation is capped at $300,000 regardless of cost overruns, and all excess costs are borne solely by Lessee.

key_clauses.Section 2 — Escrowed Ground Rent Payment: Lessor will hold nine monthly Base Rent payments of $31,666.67 (September 2024 through May 2025) plus $14,999.97 of the June 2025 payment in a segregated Escrow Account, totaling $300,000.00. Lessor retains all interest earned on the Escrowed Funds and may transfer that interest to its own accounts at any time.
key_clauses.Section 3 — Chiller Replacement Project: The Escrowed Funds are held solely to finance the replacement of the ice arena's chiller system by contractor Everything Ice, Inc. under an AIA A104-2017 contract. Lessee must provide Lessor a copy of the executed contract within one Business Day and all subsequent change orders and notices promptly.
key_clauses.Section 4 — Conditions for Release of Es: Lessee must submit written disbursement requests five Business Days in advance with invoice, sworn contractor statement, and lien waivers; if the Outstanding Balance exceeds Escrowed Funds, Lessor has sole discretion to refuse, limit, or condition disbursement. Escrowed Funds may not be used for any purpose other than the Chiller Replacement Contract without Lessor consent.
key_clauses.Section 5 — Repayment of Escrowed Funds: Lessee must repay the $300,000 Escrowed Funds plus interest at 5.125% per annum in 48 equal monthly installments of $7,531.25 beginning September 1, 2025, with any unpaid balance due in full on August 1, 2029. Early repayment relieves Lessee of interest for months following the month of full repayment, provided no Default exists.
key_clauses.Section 6 — Other Terms: Escrow Account and Escrowed Funds are Lessor's property until disbursed; Lessee and Rink Contractor have no lien rights on the account. Lessor's maximum escrow obligation is capped at $300,000 regardless of cost overruns, and all cost overruns are Lessee's sole responsibility.
key_clauses.Section 7 — WHA and WHC Guaranty: WHA and WHC remain obligated under the Existing Guaranty (dated September 1, 2012) as extended by the Joinder to cover Fifth Amendment obligations, including the Chiller Replacement Contract performance and repayment of Escrowed Funds. Each guarantor is liable for only 50% of obligations and is not jointly and severally liable.

UMB updates its notice address to Minneapolis, MN, attention Lorna Gleason, Senior Vice President, Special Accounts.

Lessor has sole and unrestricted discretion to refuse disbursement or impose conditions if the Outstanding Balance exceeds Escrowed Funds — leaves Lessee exposed if project costs overrun.
All cost overruns beyond $300,000 are entirely Lessee's responsibility with no lessor contribution, despite Lessor benefiting from capital improvements to the leased premises (chiller system).
This is the FIFTH amendment providing rent relief to Lessee, indicating a persistent pattern of financial distress and repeated reliance on Lessor accommodation since 2010.
Relief triggers include construction defects, cost overruns, unexpectedly high taxes, operating losses, COVID-19, and now a major capital equipment failure — suggesting chronic undercapitalization.
WHA and WHC guarantors are each only 50% liable and not jointly and severally liable — Lessor's remedies may be limited if one guarantor defaults on its share.
Substantial Completion of chiller system must occur by August 1, 2025 — a relatively tight deadline for a major HVAC/refrigeration project that could trigger default if missed.
Repayment of $300,000 at 5.125% interest ($7,531.25/month) begins September 1, 2025 and is in addition to regular Base Rent — significantly increases Lessee's monthly cash obligations.
WHC notary commission (Sharon M. Weber) expires December 3, 2024 — very close to execution date of October 7, 2024; raises minor procedural concern.

Lessor can use Escrowed Funds to cure any Default by Lessee upon occurrence, effectively converting rent deferral into a Lessor-controlled cure mechanism.

key_clauses.Section 3.1 — Exclusive Exempt Purpose: The Company is organized exclusively to operate the Ice Arena in support of youth hockey, figure skating, and skating appreciation as a 501(c)(3) charitable purpose. No earnings may inure to any Member or Person that is not also a 501(c)(3) Entity, and the Company may not engage in political activity or substantial lobbying.
key_clauses.Section 4.3 — Member Requirements: All Members must at all times qualify as 501(c)(3) Entities, must provide 30 days notice if that status is at risk, and must immediately relinquish their Membership Interest without compensation if they cease to so qualify. Members are affirmatively obligated to vigorously enforce their rights under the Agreement.
key_clauses.Section 5.2 — Board Composition and Voti: The Board consists of six Managers, with WHC and WHA each appointing three. Standard actions require a majority of each bloc's Managers; officer positions rotate between the two Member groups on a two-year alternating cycle.
key_clauses.Section 7.3 — Unanimous Vote Requirement: A broad range of significant actions—including any capital expenditure over $5,000, capital calls, debt incurrence, Ground Lease amendments, Bond term amendments, and membership transfers—require unanimous Board approval, giving either Member effective veto power over major decisions.
key_clauses.Section 7.4 — Deadlock Provision: If the Board deadlocks, it has 10 days to agree on a resolution procedure; if unresolved, the Presidents of each Member must submit the dispute to the Chicago International Dispute Resolution Association for mediation within 21 days, to conclude within 21 days of the first session.
key_clauses.Section 7.8 — Triggering Event Governanc: Upon a Triggering Event by a Member, that Member loses all voting rights (as Member and Manager) with respect to enforcement of the Agreement against it, and such decisions become exclusively controlled by the non-defaulting Member.
key_clauses.Section 10.2 — First Option to Remaining: Any voluntary transfer of Membership Interest must first be offered to the Remaining Member on the same terms as offered to any third party, with a 30-day acceptance window before a Permitted Transfer may proceed.
key_clauses.Section 11.1 — Ice Time Commitments: Each Member commits to minimum annual ice time purchases per 10-year agreements (Exhibit A), with reductions capped at 10% per year and 30% over any five-year period; non-payment cured within 30 days triggers the Other Member's right to sell up to 25% of the delinquent Member's committed ice time.
key_clauses.Section 12.1 — Anti-Poaching: Each Member must adopt and present to the Board a formal anti-poaching policy prohibiting recruitment of players from the other Member's programs at any level without mutual board consent.
key_clauses.Section 14.2 — Involuntary Transfer: Upon an Involuntary Transfer of a Member's Interest, the Remaining Member may purchase it for $10,000 within 60 days; if not exercised, the interest vests automatically in the Company.
key_clauses.Section 15.2 — Dissolution and Distribut: Upon dissolution, assets are liquidated, liabilities discharged, and remaining assets distributed equally among Members or to another 501(c)(3) Entity as determined by the Board, consistent with the Company's exempt purposes.
key_clauses.Section 17.5 — Amendments: Any amendment to the Agreement requires unanimous written consent of all Members and must not modify the Company's 501(c)(3) status or conflict with Section 501(c)(3) requirements.
